response: Since May 2018, the EU’s General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) has applied to any organization handling the personal data of EU residents, granting rights like access, erasure, portability and requiring explicit consent, while mandating breach reporting within 72 hours and appointing Data Protection Officers for large-scale processing. Non-compliance can trigger fines of up to 4% of global annual revenue or €20 million, and major penalties have already hit Meta (€1.2 billion in 2023), Amazon (€746 million) and Google (over €200 million). GDPR’s framework has inspired laws such as California’s CCPA, Brazil’s LGPD and India’s Digital Personal Data Protection Act, though critics argue it imposes heavy compliance costs on small businesses, stifles innovation and leads to user frustration over cookie banners amid uneven enforcement. Despite these challenges, GDPR firmly established data privacy as a fundamental right and set the global standard for data protection.
